questions.question: Match each historical figure to their contribution.
questions.explanation: Gall founded phrenology; Cajal established the neuron doctrine; Skinner was a leading behaviourist; Penfield mapped the somatosensory/motor cortex creating the homunculus; Chomsky's critique of behaviourism helped trigger the cognitive revolution.

questions.question: Match each brain structure to its primary function.
questions.explanation: The hippocampus is essential for forming new memories; the amygdala processes emotions (especially fear); the thalamus relays sensory information; the cerebellum coordinates movement; the cerebral cortex supports higher cognitive functions.

questions.question: Match each neuron part to its function.
questions.explanation: Dendrites receive input from other neurons; the soma integrates these signals; the axon carries action potentials to the terminal; and the synapse is where chemical neurotransmitters cross to the next neuron.

questions.question: Match each historical era to its defining characteristic.
categories: Stimulus-response focus, mind as black box
Return to studying internal mental processes

correct_mapping.Behaviourism: Stimulus-response focus, mind as black box
correct_mapping.Cognitive Revolution: Return to studying internal mental processes

questions.explanation: Natural Philosophy combined philosophical inquiry with emerging experimental methods; Behaviourism rejected internal states in favour of stimulus-response; the Cognitive Revolution brought back mental representations; Modern Cognitive Neuroscience uses neuroimaging to ground cognitive theories in biology.

questions.question: Match each concept to the correct description.
categories: Combining genetic and brain imaging data

correct_mapping.Neuroimaging genomics: Combining genetic and brain imaging data

questions.explanation: Polymorphisms are naturally occurring genetic variations; APOE4 is a specific allele associated with increased Alzheimer's risk; neuroimaging genomics combines genetics with brain imaging; multimethodological synthesis is the approach of combining multiple methods.

questions.question: Match each staining/mapping technique to the correct person.
items: Used Golgi stain to prove neuron doctrine
questions.explanation: Golgi developed the staining method; Cajal used it to prove neurons are discrete cells; Penfield used electrical stimulation to map the cortex; Brodmann and Semendeferi studied prefrontal cortex comparative anatomy.

questions.question: Match each approach to what it studies or emphasises.
questions.explanation: Cognitive science studies mental processes; neuroscience studies the nervous system; cognitive neuroscience bridges both to create neurobiologically grounded models; phrenology (now discredited) tried to link skull shape to mental faculties.

questions.question: Match each signal type to its location in neural communication.
questions.explanation: Action potentials propagate along the axon; neurotransmitters are released into the synaptic cleft; postsynaptic potentials occur at the receiving neuron's membrane; dendritic input arrives at the dendrites of the postsynaptic neuron.

questions.question: Match the criticism to the correct historical approach.
items: Skull bumps don't reflect brain function
questions.explanation: Phrenology's skull-reading had no validity; behaviourism deliberately ignored the mind; simple brain mapping just finds correlates without explanatory models; early introspection methods lacked replicability.

questions.question: Match each imaging-related concept to its description.
questions.explanation: fMRI is a non-invasive imaging technique; active neurons consume more energy; increased blood flow delivers oxygen to active regions; and the BOLD signal detects changes in blood oxygenation to infer neural activity.
